# COMP382 HW6: Network Flow

## Problem 1

### (a) True

Let \(f\) be any maximum flow. If the subgraph of edges carrying positive flow contains a directed cycle \(C\), define
\[
\delta = \min_{e \in C} f(e).
\]
Subtract \(\delta\) from the flow on every edge of \(C\). This keeps all edge flows nonnegative, does not violate capacities, preserves flow conservation at every vertex on the cycle, and does not change the value of the flow. At least one edge on \(C\) now has flow \(0\), so the number of positive-flow cycle edges decreases.

Repeating this process eventually removes all directed cycles of positive flow. The resulting flow is still maximum, and it contains no cycle on which every edge has positive flow.

### (b) False

Distinct capacities do not imply a unique maximum flow.

Counterexample: let the network have edges
\[
s \to a \text{ with capacity } 2,\quad s \to b \text{ with capacity } 5,\quad a \to t \text{ with capacity } 4,
\]
\[
a \to b \text{ with capacity } 3,\quad b \to t \text{ with capacity } 6.
\]
All capacities are distinct: \(2,3,4,5,6\).

The cut leaving \(s\) has capacity \(2+5=7\), so every flow has value at most \(7\). But there are at least two different flows of value \(7\):

1. \(f_1(s,a)=2,\ f_1(a,t)=2,\ f_1(s,b)=5,\ f_1(b,t)=5,\ f_1(a,b)=0\).
2. \(f_2(s,a)=2,\ f_2(a,t)=1,\ f_2(a,b)=1,\ f_2(s,b)=5,\ f_2(b,t)=6\).

Both are feasible and both have value \(7\), so both are maximum flows. Therefore the maximum flow need not be unique.

### (c) True

For any cut \(C\), if every edge capacity is multiplied by a positive constant \(\lambda\), then the cut capacity becomes
\[
c'(C) = \sum_{e \in C} \lambda c(e) = \lambda c(C).
\]
Since \(\lambda > 0\), multiplication by \(\lambda\) preserves the ordering of all cut capacities. Thus the cut (or set of cuts) that was minimum before scaling is still minimum after scaling. So the minimum cut is unchanged.

### (d) False

Adding the same positive amount to every edge can change which cut is minimum, because cuts with different numbers of crossing edges increase by different amounts.

Counterexample: let the graph have edges
\[
s \to a \text{ with capacity } 1,\quad s \to t \text{ with capacity } 1,\quad a \to t \text{ with capacity } 3.
\]
Consider the two \(s\)-\(t\) cuts:

1. \(S=\{s\}\), whose capacity is \(1+1=2\).
2. \(S=\{s,a\}\), whose capacity is \(3\).

Originally the minimum cut is \(S=\{s\}\).

Now add \(\lambda=2\) to every edge. The capacities become \(3,3,5\). Then:

1. \(S=\{s\}\) has capacity \(3+3=6\).
2. \(S=\{s,a\}\) has capacity \(5\).

So the minimum cut changes to \(S=\{s,a\}\). Hence the statement is false.

## Problem 2

Let \(\lambda\) be the value of a maximum \(s\)-\(t\) flow in the original graph. Because every edge has unit capacity, \(\lambda\) is also the size of a minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut.

### Algorithm

1. Compute a maximum flow in \(G\).
2. Extract any minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut \(C\) from the residual graph. Then \(|C|=\lambda\).
3. If \(k \le \lambda\), delete any \(k\) edges from \(C\).
4. If \(k > \lambda\), delete all edges of \(C\), and delete any additional \(k-\lambda\) edges arbitrarily.

### Correctness

Take any set \(F\) of exactly \(k\) deleted edges, and let \(\lambda'\) be the maximum flow value after deleting \(F\). For any \(s\)-\(t\) cut \(D\) in the original graph,
\[
|D \setminus F| = |D| - |D \cap F| \ge \lambda - k,
\]
because \(|D| \ge \lambda\) and \(|D \cap F| \le k\).

This is true for every cut \(D\), so every cut in the new graph has capacity at least \(\lambda-k\). Therefore
\[
\lambda' \ge \max(\lambda-k,0).
\]

Now consider the algorithm's choice. If \(k \le \lambda\), we delete \(k\) edges from a minimum cut \(C\). The remaining capacity of that cut is
\[
|C|-k = \lambda-k.
\]
Hence the new minimum cut has capacity at most \(\lambda-k\), so \(\lambda' \le \lambda-k\). Together with the lower bound above, this gives \(\lambda'=\lambda-k\), which is optimal.

If \(k > \lambda\), deleting all edges of the minimum cut disconnects \(s\) from \(t\), so the new maximum flow is \(0\), which is clearly optimal.

Thus an optimal solution is obtained by deleting as many edges as possible from any minimum cut.

### Running Time

The algorithm performs one max-flow computation and one min-cut extraction, both polynomial-time operations. Therefore the overall algorithm runs in polynomial time, with no dependence on \(k\) in the exponent.

## Problem 3

Reduce the node-capacitated problem to an ordinary edge-capacitated max-flow problem by splitting each vertex.

### Construction

For each vertex \(v \in V\), create two vertices \(v_{\text{in}}\) and \(v_{\text{out}}\), and add an edge
\[
v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}
\]
with capacity \(c_v\).

For each original directed edge \((u,v) \in E\), add an edge
\[
u_{\text{out}} \to v_{\text{in}}.
\]
If the original network also has edge capacities, assign this new edge the same capacity. If the original edges have no capacities, assign it capacity \(+\infty\) (or any sufficiently large number).

Use \(s_{\text{out}}\) as the source and \(t_{\text{in}}\) as the sink. If \(s\) and \(t\) are not meant to be bounded by node capacities, set the capacities of \(s_{\text{in}} \to s_{\text{out}}\) and \(t_{\text{in}} \to t_{\text{out}}\) to \(+\infty\).

### Correctness

Any feasible flow in the original graph corresponds to a feasible flow in the transformed graph: whenever flow passes through a vertex \(v\), it must traverse the edge \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\), whose capacity is \(c_v\). Therefore no more than \(c_v\) units can pass through \(v\).

Conversely, any flow in the transformed graph induces a flow in the original graph, because the only way to move through \(v\) is across the edge \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\), which enforces the node-capacity constraint. Thus feasible flows in the two networks are in one-to-one correspondence and have the same value.

Therefore computing a maximum flow in the transformed network yields a maximum feasible flow in the original node-capacitated network.

### Running Time

The transformed graph has \(2|V|\) vertices and \(|V|+|E|\) edges, so one standard max-flow computation solves the problem in polynomial time.

## Problem 4

We want the smallest number of stations whose removal disconnects \(A\) from \(B\), without removing \(A\) or \(B\). This is a minimum vertex-cut problem.

### Construction

Create a directed network by splitting every station \(v\) into \(v_{\text{in}}\) and \(v_{\text{out}}\), with an edge
\[
v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}.
\]

Assign capacities as follows:

1. If \(v \neq A,B\), give \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\) capacity \(1\).
2. If \(v=A\) or \(v=B\), give \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\) capacity \(+\infty\).

For each undirected rail edge \(\{u,v\}\), add the two directed edges
\[
u_{\text{out}} \to v_{\text{in}}
\quad\text{and}\quad
v_{\text{out}} \to u_{\text{in}},
\]
each with capacity \(+\infty\).

Set the source to \(A_{\text{out}}\) and the sink to \(B_{\text{in}}\).

### Correctness

Any finite-capacity cut in this network cannot use an \(+\infty\)-capacity edge, so a minimum cut can only use edges of the form
\[
v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}
\]
for stations \(v \neq A,B\). Cutting such an edge means removing station \(v\), and each removed station contributes exactly \(1\) to the cut capacity.

Therefore:

1. Every set of closed stations that blocks all \(A\)-to-\(B\) travel gives a cut of the same size.
2. Every minimum cut gives a minimum-size set of stations whose closure blocks all travel from \(A\) to \(B\).

So a minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut in the constructed network gives exactly the minimum number of stations to close.

### Running Time

The constructed network has \(2|V|\) vertices and \(|V| + 2|E|\) edges, so one max-flow/min-cut computation solves the problem in polynomial time.

## Problem 5

Model the problem as a max-flow instance on a bipartite graph between classes and room-time pairs.

### Construction

Create the following nodes:

1. A source \(s\).
2. One node \(C_i\) for each class \(i=1,\dots,n\).
3. One node \(R_{j,\ell}\) for each room \(j=1,\dots,r\) and time slot \(\ell=1,\dots,t\).
4. A sink \(t\).

Add edges:

1. \(s \to C_i\) with capacity \(1\) for every class \(i\).
2. \(C_i \to R_{j,\ell}\) with capacity \(1\) whenever class \(i\) fits in room \(j\), i.e. whenever \(E[i] < S[j]\).
3. \(R_{j,\ell} \to t\) with capacity \(1\) for every room-time pair \((j,\ell)\).

### Correctness

If there is a feasible exam schedule, then for each class \(i\) assigned to room \(j\) at time slot \(\ell\), send one unit of flow along
\[
s \to C_i \to R_{j,\ell} \to t.
\]
Because each class is scheduled once and each room-time pair is used at most once, this produces a feasible flow of value \(n\).

Conversely, if the maximum flow has value \(n\), then each class node \(C_i\) must send exactly one unit of flow to some room-time node \(R_{j,\ell}\). Since all capacities are \(1\), no room-time node can receive more than one unit. Therefore the flow directly defines a valid assignment of every class to one room and one time slot.

So a feasible schedule exists if and only if the maximum flow value is \(n\). If the max flow value is smaller than \(n\), then no valid assignment exists.

### Running Time

The network has
\[
n + rt + 2
\]
vertices. In the worst case, every class fits in every room, so the number of edges is
\[
n + nrt + rt = O(nrt).
\]
Thus one max-flow computation solves the problem in polynomial time. Equivalently, this is a bipartite matching problem between \(n\) class nodes and \(rt\) room-time nodes.

graderReview.content:
# COMP382 HW6: Network Flow — LLM Grading Results

## Results Summary

**Passed:** 5/5 problems (100%)  
**Points:** 100/100

## Detailed Results

### ✅ Problem 1: True or False (20/20 pts)

This is a **fully correct** solution. In part **(a)**, your cycle-cancellation proof is complete: subtracting the minimum flow on the directed cycle preserves feasibility and flow value, and repeating eliminates all positive-flow cycles. In part **(b)**, your counterexample is valid and well checked; you correctly verify both feasibility and optimality by comparing to the cut out of `s`. In part **(c)**, your argument that multiplying all cut values by the same positive constant preserves their ordering is exactly the right justification. In part **(d)**, your counterexample is clean and directly illustrates the key issue: adding `\lambda` changes a cut's value by `\lambda` times the number of crossing edges, so cuts with different sizes can swap order.

Compared with the reference solution, your answers are mathematically equivalent and just as rigorous.

### ✅ Problem 2: Flow Reduction (20/20 pts)

This solution is **correct and complete**. You correctly use the fact that in a unit-capacity graph, the max-flow value `\lambda` equals the size of a minimum cut. The core proof is strong: for **any** deleted edge set `F` of size `k`, every cut loses at most `k` edges, so every remaining cut has capacity at least `\lambda-k` (or at least `0` when `k > \lambda`). That gives the optimal lower bound. Then you show that deleting `k` edges from an actual minimum cut achieves exactly that bound, so the strategy is optimal.

This is essentially the same argument as the reference solution, just written more explicitly. The runtime statement is also sufficient for full credit, since the assignment only asked for polynomial time without dependence on `k` in the exponent.

### ✅ Problem 3: Node Capacities (20/20 pts)

This is a **correct reduction**. Splitting each vertex `v` into `v_in` and `v_out` with a capacity-`c_v` edge between them is exactly the standard transformation. You also correctly route every original edge `(u,v)` as `u_out -> v_in`, and your discussion of using `+\infty` (or a sufficiently large finite number) for unrestricted edges is appropriate.

Your correctness argument covers both directions: any feasible original flow can be realized in the transformed graph, and any transformed flow induces a feasible original flow with the same value. That is the essential proof. The complexity discussion is also fine.

One small note: since the original problem only specifies node capacities, mentioning optional original edge capacities is extra, but it does not hurt correctness.

### ✅ Problem 4: Station Closing (20/20 pts)

This solution is **fully correct**. You identify the problem as a minimum vertex-cut problem and then apply the standard node-splitting reduction. Giving capacity `1` to each ordinary station and `+\infty` to `A` and `B` correctly models the requirement that only intermediate stations may be closed. Converting each undirected rail edge into both directed directions is also correct.

Your correctness explanation is solid: any finite minimum cut must avoid the infinite-capacity travel edges and the protected `A`/`B` edges, so it consists exactly of internal station edges, each corresponding to closing one station. That is exactly the relationship the reference solution uses.

### ✅ Problem 5: Exam Scheduling (20/20 pts)

This is a **correct** solution, even though it differs from the reference construction. Your network with one node for each room-time pair is a natural and fully valid modeling choice. It enforces exactly what the problem requires: each class is assigned once, and each room can host at most one exam in each time slot because each room-time pair has capacity `1` into the sink.

The correctness proof is complete in both directions. A valid schedule gives a flow of value `n`, and a flow of value `n` directly decodes to a valid schedule. The runtime is also polynomial. In fact, your construction is arguably more direct than the reference solution's layered room/time design.

Compared with the reference solution, this deserves full credit because it is an equivalent max-flow formulation.

## Overall Comments

The submission is **consistently strong across all five problems**. The main pattern is that you chose the right max-flow/min-cut formulations and then justified them with clean, mathematically sound arguments. In particular:

- Your true/false justifications are rigorous rather than intuitive.
- Your flow reduction arguments in Problems 2, 3, and 4 are correct and complete.
- In Problem 5, you used a different construction from the reference solution, but it is fully valid and earns full credit.

There are no substantive correctness issues. The work is concise but still includes the key proof ideas, which is exactly what is needed in this course.

---

This is a **correct** solution, even though it differs from the reference construction. Your network with one node for each room-time pair is a natural and fully valid modeling choice. It enforces exactly what the problem requires: each class is assigned once, and each room can host at most one exam in each time slot because each room-time pair has capacity `1` into the sink.

The correctness proof is complete in both directions. A valid schedule gives a flow of value `n`, and a flow of value `n` directly decodes to a valid schedule. The runtime is also polynomial. In fact, your construction is arguably more direct than the reference solution's layered room/time design.

Compared with the reference solution, this deserves full credit because it is an equivalent max-flow formulation.

## Overall Comments

The submission is **consistently strong across all five problems**. The main pattern is that you chose the right max-flow/min-cut formulations and then justified them with clean, mathematically sound arguments. In particular:

- Your true/false justifications are rigorous rather than intuitive.
- Your flow reduction arguments in Problems 2, 3, and 4 are correct and complete.
- In Problem 5, you used a different construction from the reference solution, but it is fully valid and earns full credit.

There are no substantive correctness issues. The work is concise but still includes the key proof ideas, which is exactly what is needed in this course.

# COMP382 HW6: Network Flow — LLM Grading Results

## Results Summary

**Passed:** 5/5 problems (97%)  
**Points:** 97/100

## Detailed Results

### ✅ Problem 1: True or False (18/20 pts)

Parts (a)-(c) are excellent. Part (a)'s proof is actually more thorough than the reference solution, clearly explaining the iterative removal process. Part (b) provides a clean, verifiable counterexample.

The issue is in **part (d)**. While the answer (FALSE) and the intuition are correct, the counterexample contains an error: when computing the capacity of cut S={s,a}, you forgot to include the edge s→t, which goes from s∈S to t∈T. The correct capacity of that cut is 1+3=4, not 3. After adding λ=2, both cuts scale differently but the minimum cut remains {s} (capacity 6 vs 8), so this counterexample does not demonstrate a change in the minimum cut.

A valid counterexample requires a graph where a cut with *fewer* crossing edges beats one with *more* crossing edges after the additive shift — the reference solution uses a 4-node graph to achieve this.

> **Errors:** Part (d): The counterexample has a calculation error. The cut S={s,a} in the graph with edges s→a (cap 1), s→t (cap 1), a→t (cap 3) should include BOTH the s→t edge and the a→t edge (since both cross from S to T), giving capacity 1+3=4, not 3. With the correct capacities (original: {s}=2, {s,a}=4; after +2: {s}=6, {s,a}=8), the minimum cut does NOT change, so the counterexample is invalid.

### ✅ Problem 2: Flow Reduction (20/20 pts)

This is an excellent solution that matches the reference in both approach and rigor. The optimality proof is particularly well-structured: you establish a universal lower bound (no deletion of k edges can reduce the flow below max(λ-k, 0)) and then show the algorithm achieves exactly this bound. The handling of both cases (k ≤ λ and k > λ) is complete. The observation that λ equals the min-cut size in a unit-capacity graph is correctly leveraged throughout.

### ✅ Problem 3: Node Capacities (20/20 pts)

This is a complete and correct solution that matches the reference. The construction is standard and well-explained. The correctness argument clearly establishes the bijection between feasible flows in the two networks. The additional detail about handling s and t node capacities shows good understanding of the edge cases. The complexity analysis is correct.

### ✅ Problem 4: Station Closing (20/20 pts)

Excellent solution that matches the reference. The key insight — using ∞ capacity for A_in→A_out and B_in→B_out to prevent closing those stations — is correctly applied. The construction ensures that any finite min cut can only cut internal vertex edges for non-A, non-B stations, and each such cut edge corresponds to closing exactly one station. The correctness argument is well-structured, and the complexity analysis is correct.

### ✅ Problem 5: Exam Scheduling (19/20 pts)

The solution uses a valid alternative construction compared to the reference. Instead of having separate room and time node layers (which yields a smaller graph with O(nr + rt) edges), the student creates combined room-time pair nodes, resulting in O(nrt) edges. This is correct but produces a larger graph. The correctness proof properly establishes equivalence between max flow of value n and a feasible schedule.

The only gap is in the complexity statement: the problem explicitly asks to "state and briefly explain the complexity," but the student only says "polynomial time" and gives the graph dimensions without computing the final expression. A complete answer would state something like O((n + rt) · (nrt)²) using Edmonds-Karp, or identify the equivalent bipartite matching bound.

> **Errors:** Complexity analysis is incomplete: the problem asks to 'State and briefly explain the complexity' but the student only says 'polynomial time' without giving an explicit expression such as O(VE²) = O((n+rt)(nrt)²) for Edmonds-Karp

 # COMP382 HW6: Network Flow
 
-## Student Responses
+## Problem 1
 
-<!-- Write your solutions below. Add sections for each problem as needed. -->
-<!-- Use proper mathematical notation where applicable. -->
+### (a) True
 
-[Your solutions here]
+Let \(f\) be any maximum flow. If the subgraph of edges carrying positive flow contains a directed cycle \(C\), define
+\[
+\delta = \min_{e \in C} f(e).
+\]
+Subtract \(\delta\) from the flow on every edge of \(C\). This keeps all edge flows nonnegative, does not violate capacities, preserves flow conservation at every vertex on the cycle, and does not change the value of the flow. At least one edge on \(C\) now has flow \(0\), so the number of positive-flow cycle edges decreases.
 
+Repeating this process eventually removes all directed cycles of positive flow. The resulting flow is still maximum, and it contains no cycle on which every edge has positive flow.
+
+### (b) False
+
+Distinct capacities do not imply a unique maximum flow.
+
+Counterexample: let the network have edges
+\[
+s \to a \text{ with capacity } 2,\quad s \to b \text{ with capacity } 5,\quad a \to t \text{ with capacity } 4,
+\]
+\[
+a \to b \text{ with capacity } 3,\quad b \to t \text{ with capacity } 6.
+\]
+All capacities are distinct: \(2,3,4,5,6\).
+
+The cut leaving \(s\) has capacity \(2+5=7\), so every flow has value at most \(7\). But there are at least two different flows of value \(7\):
+
+1. \(f_1(s,a)=2,\ f_1(a,t)=2,\ f_1(s,b)=5,\ f_1(b,t)=5,\ f_1(a,b)=0\).
+2. \(f_2(s,a)=2,\ f_2(a,t)=1,\ f_2(a,b)=1,\ f_2(s,b)=5,\ f_2(b,t)=6\).
+
+Both are feasible and both have value \(7\), so both are maximum flows. Therefore the maximum flow need not be unique.
+
+### (c) True
+
+For any cut \(C\), if every edge capacity is multiplied by a positive constant \(\lambda\), then the cut capacity becomes
+\[
+c'(C) = \sum_{e \in C} \lambda c(e) = \lambda c(C).
+\]
+Since \(\lambda > 0\), multiplication by \(\lambda\) preserves the ordering of all cut capacities. Thus the cut (or set of cuts) that was minimum before scaling is still minimum after scaling. So the minimum cut is unchanged.
+
+### (d) False
+
+Adding the same positive amount to every edge can change which cut is minimum, because cuts with different numbers of crossing edges increase by different amounts.
+
+Counterexample: let the graph have edges
+\[
+s \to a \text{ with capacity } 1,\quad s \to t \text{ with capacity } 1,\quad a \to t \text{ with capacity } 3.
+\]
+Consider the two \(s\)-\(t\) cuts:
+
+1. \(S=\{s\}\), whose capacity is \(1+1=2\).
+2. \(S=\{s,a\}\), whose capacity is \(3\).
+
+Originally the minimum cut is \(S=\{s\}\).
+
+Now add \(\lambda=2\) to every edge. The capacities become \(3,3,5\). Then:
+
+1. \(S=\{s\}\) has capacity \(3+3=6\).
+2. \(S=\{s,a\}\) has capacity \(5\).
+
+So the minimum cut changes to \(S=\{s,a\}\). Hence the statement is false.
+
+## Problem 2
+
+Let \(\lambda\) be the value of a maximum \(s\)-\(t\) flow in the original graph. Because every edge has unit capacity, \(\lambda\) is also the size of a minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut.
+
+### Algorithm
+
+1. Compute a maximum flow in \(G\).
+2. Extract any minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut \(C\) from the residual graph. Then \(|C|=\lambda\).
+3. If \(k \le \lambda\), delete any \(k\) edges from \(C\).
+4. If \(k > \lambda\), delete all edges of \(C\), and delete any additional \(k-\lambda\) edges arbitrarily.
+
+### Correctness
+
+Take any set \(F\) of exactly \(k\) deleted edges, and let \(\lambda'\) be the maximum flow value after deleting \(F\). For any \(s\)-\(t\) cut \(D\) in the original graph,
+\[
+|D \setminus F| = |D| - |D \cap F| \ge \lambda - k,
+\]
+because \(|D| \ge \lambda\) and \(|D \cap F| \le k\).
+
+This is true for every cut \(D\), so every cut in the new graph has capacity at least \(\lambda-k\). Therefore
+\[
+\lambda' \ge \max(\lambda-k,0).
+\]
+
+Now consider the algorithm's choice. If \(k \le \lambda\), we delete \(k\) edges from a minimum cut \(C\). The remaining capacity of that cut is
+\[
+|C|-k = \lambda-k.
+\]
+Hence the new minimum cut has capacity at most \(\lambda-k\), so \(\lambda' \le \lambda-k\). Together with the lower bound above, this gives \(\lambda'=\lambda-k\), which is optimal.
+
+If \(k > \lambda\), deleting all edges of the minimum cut disconnects \(s\) from \(t\), so the new maximum flow is \(0\), which is clearly optimal.
+
+Thus an optimal solution is obtained by deleting as many edges as possible from any minimum cut.
+
+### Running Time
+
+The algorithm performs one max-flow computation and one min-cut extraction, both polynomial-time operations. Therefore the overall algorithm runs in polynomial time, with no dependence on \(k\) in the exponent.
+
+## Problem 3
+
+Reduce the node-capacitated problem to an ordinary edge-capacitated max-flow problem by splitting each vertex.
+
+### Construction
+
+For each vertex \(v \in V\), create two vertices \(v_{\text{in}}\) and \(v_{\text{out}}\), and add an edge
+\[
+v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}
+\]
+with capacity \(c_v\).
+
+For each original directed edge \((u,v) \in E\), add an edge
+\[
+u_{\text{out}} \to v_{\text{in}}.
+\]
+If the original network also has edge capacities, assign this new edge the same capacity. If the original edges have no capacities, assign it capacity \(+\infty\) (or any sufficiently large number).
+
+Use \(s_{\text{out}}\) as the source and \(t_{\text{in}}\) as the sink. If \(s\) and \(t\) are not meant to be bounded by node capacities, set the capacities of \(s_{\text{in}} \to s_{\text{out}}\) and \(t_{\text{in}} \to t_{\text{out}}\) to \(+\infty\).
+
+### Correctness
+
+Any feasible flow in the original graph corresponds to a feasible flow in the transformed graph: whenever flow passes through a vertex \(v\), it must traverse the edge \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\), whose capacity is \(c_v\). Therefore no more than \(c_v\) units can pass through \(v\).
+
+Conversely, any flow in the transformed graph induces a flow in the original graph, because the only way to move through \(v\) is across the edge \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\), which enforces the node-capacity constraint. Thus feasible flows in the two networks are in one-to-one correspondence and have the same value.
+
+Therefore computing a maximum flow in the transformed network yields a maximum feasible flow in the original node-capacitated network.
+
+### Running Time
+
+The transformed graph has \(2|V|\) vertices and \(|V|+|E|\) edges, so one standard max-flow computation solves the problem in polynomial time.
+
+## Problem 4
+
+We want the smallest number of stations whose removal disconnects \(A\) from \(B\), without removing \(A\) or \(B\). This is a minimum vertex-cut problem.
+
+### Construction
+
+Create a directed network by splitting every station \(v\) into \(v_{\text{in}}\) and \(v_{\text{out}}\), with an edge
+\[
+v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}.
+\]
+
+Assign capacities as follows:
+
+1. If \(v \neq A,B\), give \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\) capacity \(1\).
+2. If \(v=A\) or \(v=B\), give \(v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}\) capacity \(+\infty\).
+
+For each undirected rail edge \(\{u,v\}\), add the two directed edges
+\[
+u_{\text{out}} \to v_{\text{in}}
+\quad\text{and}\quad
+v_{\text{out}} \to u_{\text{in}},
+\]
+each with capacity \(+\infty\).
+
+Set the source to \(A_{\text{out}}\) and the sink to \(B_{\text{in}}\).
+
+### Correctness
+
+Any finite-capacity cut in this network cannot use an \(+\infty\)-capacity edge, so a minimum cut can only use edges of the form
+\[
+v_{\text{in}} \to v_{\text{out}}
+\]
+for stations \(v \neq A,B\). Cutting such an edge means removing station \(v\), and each removed station contributes exactly \(1\) to the cut capacity.
+
+Therefore:
+
+1. Every set of closed stations that blocks all \(A\)-to-\(B\) travel gives a cut of the same size.
+2. Every minimum cut gives a minimum-size set of stations whose closure blocks all travel from \(A\) to \(B\).
+
+So a minimum \(s\)-\(t\) cut in the constructed network gives exactly the minimum number of stations to close.
+
+### Running Time
+
+The constructed network has \(2|V|\) vertices and \(|V| + 2|E|\) edges, so one max-flow/min-cut computation solves the problem in polynomial time.
+
+## Problem 5
+
+Model the problem as a max-flow instance on a bipartite graph between classes and room-time pairs.
+
+### Construction
+
+Create the following nodes:
+
+1. A source \(s\).
+2. One node \(C_i\) for each class \(i=1,\dots,n\).
+3. One node \(R_{j,\ell}\) for each room \(j=1,\dots,r\) and time slot \(\ell=1,\dots,t\).
+4. A sink \(t\).
+
+Add edges:
+
+1. \(s \to C_i\) with capacity \(1\) for every class \(i\).
+2. \(C_i \to R_{j,\ell}\) with capacity \(1\) whenever class \(i\) fits in room \(j\), i.e. whenever \(E[i] < S[j]\).
+3. \(R_{j,\ell} \to t\) with capacity \(1\) for every room-time pair \((j,\ell)\).
+
+### Correctness
+
+If there is a feasible exam schedule, then for each class \(i\) assigned to room \(j\) at time slot \(\ell\), send one unit of flow along
+\[
+s \to C_i \to R_{j,\ell} \to t.
+\]
+Because each class is scheduled once and each room-time pair is used at most once, this produces a feasible flow of value \(n\).
+
+Conversely, if the maximum flow has value \(n\), then each class node \(C_i\) must send exactly one unit of flow to some room-time node \(R_{j,\ell}\). Since all capacities are \(1\), no room-time node can receive more than one unit. Therefore the flow directly defines a valid assignment of every class to one room and one time slot.
+
+So a feasible schedule exists if and only if the maximum flow value is \(n\). If the max flow value is smaller than \(n\), then no valid assignment exists.
+
+### Running Time
+
+The network has
+\[
+n + rt + 2
+\]
+vertices. In the worst case, every class fits in every room, so the number of edges is
+\[
+n + nrt + rt = O(nrt).
+\]
+Thus one max-flow computation solves the problem in polynomial time. Equivalently, this is a bipartite matching problem between \(n\) class nodes and \(rt\) room-time nodes.
